kanji.meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>rice paddy</radical> you find a <radical>heart</radical> and it makes you <kanji>think</kanji>. You think a lot of <kanji>thought</kanji>s like, "Whose heart is this? Why is it in a rice paddy? Why am I thinking out loud?"
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you are <kanji>think</kanji>ing these <kanji>thought</kanji>s, you spot someone on the edge of the rice field. He really reminds you of someone. But who? That's it! He reminds you of that bartender on The Simpsons. What's his name? You think some more. What is it? <reading>Oh! Moe</reading> (おも) is his name!
kanji.scene_hook: In a rice paddy you find a heart and it makes you think.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Of course when you find a toe <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, you want to know where the toe came from. When you examine it, you see a name written on there. This toe belongs to the local clumsy farmhand, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう).
kanji.scene_hook: You find a toe on the ground.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The radical for <radical>eat</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>eat</kanji> are the same! Lucky you.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Thank goodness, you finally get to <kanji>eat</kanji> your <kanji>meal</kanji>. You're so hungry. Someone has been starving you and now they've finally given you food. Unfortunately, though, every time you take a bite, you get a <reading>shock</reading> (しょく) from somewhere.
kanji.scene_hook: The radical for eat and the kanji for eat are the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>say</radical> radical and the <kanji>say</kanji> kanji are the same!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: If you aren't sure what to <kanji>say</kanji>, just think about <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん) and ask yourself, "What would Genji say?"
kanji.scene_hook: The say radical and the say kanji are the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>coffin</radical> there's a <radical>beggar</radical>, doing his begging. But it doesn't work, nobody helps him. So, he just begins to <kanji>think</kanji>: Why am I in this situation? How could I have avoided it? How will I get out? Think, dammit, think!!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: What does the beggar <kanji>think</kanji> of? A thought bubble appears near his head. In it is a picture of <reading>こう</reading>いち! He's thinking that if こういち were here, he'd save him. Oh, if only こういち could save us all!
kanji.scene_hook: In a coffin there's a beggar, doing his begging.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You give your <radical>scooter</radical> to a <radical>turkey</radical> so that it can <kanji>advance</kanji>. The turkey was trying and failing to walk through a crowded street, so out of the goodness of your heart you gave it a scooter so it could advance.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You wave as the turkey <kanji>advance</kanji>s into the street at top speeds, collides with a car, and scrapes up its <reading>shin</reading>s (しん) almost immediately. Ah!
kanji.scene_hook: You give your scooter to a turkey so that it can advance.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> with a <radical>spoon</radical> is a powerful thing. Even something as ordinary as a spoon can create great <kanji>change</kanji> in the world when given to the right person.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Then, this hero of yours takes their spoon and says some magic words into it, <kanji>changing</kanji> it into a <reading>car</reading> (か).
kanji.scene_hook: A leader with a spoon is a powerful thing.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You see something. It's walking towards you, but you need to see better. When the person comes close, you realize "That person is <reading>me</reading> (み)!"
kanji.scene_hook: The radical see and the kanji see are exactly the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>world</radical> radical and the <kanji>world</kanji> kanji are the same, but there is another really important meaning you need to know: <kanji>generation</kanji>. 

Think of it this way: the world is just made up of many different generations of people, plants, and animals all getting along together.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: It's your job to protect the <kanji>world</kanji> and every <kanji>generation</kanji> with your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). It was given to you to uphold correctness, and you'll pass it on to the next generation when it's time. Then they'll watch out for the world when you're gone.
kanji.scene_hook: The world radical and the world kanji are the same, but there is another really important meaning you need to know: generation.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Every <radical>rice paddy</radical> sits <radical>jammed in</radical> to the <kanji>world</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: To get around this <kanji>world</kanji> of rice paddies, you need to use a <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい).
kanji.scene_hook: Every rice paddy sits jammed in to the world.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There are two <radical>mountains</radical> in front of you... and probably a bunch more around you too. You are in a pass, and don't know how to get out. You are frantically looking for the <kanji>exit</kanji> before you freeze to death. The most important thing is to find the exit from this mountain range - your life depends on it.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you're trying to <kanji>exit</kanji> this mountain range, you suddenly realize that someone is actually hunting you. What? Make sure to feel that sudden realization. It's crazy. Then, you find the exit and run towards it. Sadly, the hunter is waiting there, and he <reading>shoots you</reading> (しゅつ) through the leg, knocking you down.
kanji.scene_hook: There are two mountains in front of you...

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You take a <radical>grain</radical> and fashion it into a <radical>gun</radical> that is <radical>small</radical>, earning you the <kanji>title</kanji> of Small Arms Dealer (or SAD). Congratulations!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Word of your SAD <kanji>title</kanji> makes it all the way to the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) and he travels out to see you. When he arrives at your door, with all of his fancy escorts, he hands you a plaque with your title on it.
kanji.scene_hook: You take a grain and fashion it into a gun that is small, earning you the title of Small Arms Dealer (or SAD).

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>mouth</radical> in the <radical>ground</radical> (in our case, it's a hatch), and inside is some kind of <radical>prison</radical> you can't quite get to. If you've ever seen Lost, this will sound familiar... on the hatch, there's actually a series of <kanji>number</kanji>s that you don't understand.
kanji.reading_mnemonic:
"You want to know the <kanji>number</kanji> to the hatch?" yells an old voice from inside. You say "yes," and the voice gives you the numbers and you're in! You go down into the prison, where there is one prisoner: Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち.

Note: For the ごう reading mnemonics, we use Grandpa ごういち because こういち is used for the こう reading. So just remember that ごう comes from Koichi's grandpa ごういち.
kanji.scene_hook: There's a mouth in the ground (in our case, it's a hatch), and inside is some kind of prison you can't quite get to.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You need to get your <radical>fingers</radical> around a <radical>temple</radical> if you want to <kanji>hold</kanji> it. Is the temple too big? Hold smaller temples you can buy in the temple's souvenir shop.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>hold</kanji> the temple you hear a voice. You look down through your fingers and see a tiny <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). He's smiling and gives you a thumbs up. Aww, thanks Jesus!
kanji.scene_hook: You need to get your fingers around a temple if you want to hold it.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Below the <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s is a <radical>demon</radical>. This demon is a <kanji>devil</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Why is the <kanji>devil</kanji> under a canopy of trees? Because he's looking for his <reading>ma</reading>ma (ま).
kanji.scene_hook: Below the canopy of trees is a demon.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>s in the <radical>past</radical> changed the <kanji>method</kanji>s and <kanji>law</kanji>s of today.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: These <kanji>method</kanji>s and <kanji>law</kanji>s affected your own <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう) the most.
kanji.scene_hook: Tsunamis in the past changed the methods and laws of today.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: What is the <radical>evening</radical> <radical>mouth</radical>? Imagine a mouth that's up in the night sky. It's just there, talking, and only comes out at night. It only appears in one area of the world. (What area would you choose? Imagine yourself going to that place to see the "evening mouth.") And it appears only one month out of the year (more on this in a second). The "evening mouth" is the most famous thing in the _____ area (this is the area you choose). He's so <kanji>famous</kanji> that everybody knows his <kanji>name</kanji> ("Evening Mouth").
kanji.reading_mnemonic: So, when does “Evening Mouth” appear? Turns out, “Evening Mouth” only comes out to talk in <reading>May</reading> (めい). You know this because it’s just starting to be spring, and when you look around (you’re watching “Evening Mouth” again) you see some spring flowers come up.
kanji.scene_hook: What is the evening mouth?

kanji.meaning_mnemonic:
There are some <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> and a <radical>moon</radical> <radical>knife</radical> in <kanji>front</kanji> of you. When you try to move, they always rotate around with you, staying right in front. You can't escape these objects, for some reason.

This kanji means <kanji>before</kanji> too. Something that's in front of you is before you, after all.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You're going crazy with these things in <kanji>front</kanji> of you all the time. You finally seek the help of a <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん) master, who tells you that meditation will save you from this curse. You try it out, and sure enough, the objects are gone! It worked!
kanji.scene_hook: There are some horns on the ground and a moon knife in front of you.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>self</radical> radical and <kanji>self</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
kanji.reading_mnemonic:
When we need to remember じ we use the word "Jesus."

Who said you should always look at your <kanji>self</kanji> before tossing stones (or whatever) at others? <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) did. So, make sure you look at your own self before being a jerk.
kanji.scene_hook: The self radical and self are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>fins</radical> that fell onto my <radical>sword</radical> were sliced into many <kanji>part</kanji>s. Following this logic, this also means <kanji>minute</kanji>, which is part of an hour, and <kanji>divide</kanji>, which is the action of splitting something into many parts.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Now that the fins have been cut into neat <kanji>part</kanji>s, it's time to cut something else: <reading>bun</reading>s (ぶん).
kanji.scene_hook: The fins that fell onto my sword were sliced into many parts.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>yakuza</radical> hands you a <radical>spoon</radical>, <kanji>death</kanji> is coming for you. It's the death spoon, a sign that the yakuza is coming to cause your death.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Death</kanji> comes in the form of a <reading>shee</reading>p (し). The death sheep works for the yakuza, and kills anyone who has their spoon.
kanji.scene_hook: If the yakuza hands you a spoon, death is coming for you.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>big</radical> radical and the <kanji>big</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: There's this big guy (that's how you remember the radical, remember?), and he's wearing a huge <reading>Tie Dye</reading> (たい, だい) shirt. Because he's so huge, the tie dye shirt is also super huge. Everything about him is pretty big, but the tie dye stands out more than anything.
kanji.scene_hook: The big radical and the big kanji are exactly the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant <radical>person</radical> because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out? Well this is a <kanji>person</kanji> too!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Only one <kanji>person</kanji> at <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (にん) complained about the new <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) policy, and that person was you.
kanji.scene_hook: Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant person because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out?

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>gate</radical> that the <radical>sun</radical> goes through at a regular <kanji>interval</kanji>. There is an <kanji>interval of time</kanji> in the sense that the sun goes through it at the same time every day. And there is an <kanji>interval of space</kanji> in the sense that it has to go through the space between the gate posts.
kanji.reading_mnemonic:
This method of keeping track of <kanji>interval</kanji>s of time was invented by the great Khans of Mongolia, specifically the most famous Khan of all: Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).

Genghis Khan has had a huge impact on the world as we know it, especially the Eurasian continent of the past, so he's going to come up a lot from now on. Be prepared to get very familiar with him and his friends!
kanji.scene_hook: There's a gate that the sun goes through at a regular interval.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You take an <radical>arrow</radical> to the <radical>mouth</radical> because you <kanji>know</kanji> too much. You know some very sensitive information, and someone's sent a bow-slinging enforcer to pin your big blabbermouth shut with arrows.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: One of the things you <kanji>know</kanji> too much about is the <reading>shee</reading>p (し) plan for world domination! Yes, you know that sheep are in fact plotting to overthrow humanity and take over the world.
kanji.scene_hook: You take an arrow to the mouth because you know too much.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: When your <radical>sick</radical> <radical>mama</radical> tries to do a <radical>task</radical> she's filled with <kanji>pain</kanji>!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Your mama's <kanji>pain</kanji> is <reading>too</reading> (つう) much for her to handle, so now you have to do all of her tasks too.
kanji.scene_hook: When your sick mama tries to do a task she's filled with pain!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You keep your <radical>bow</radical> <radical>private</radical> because it's made of <radical>insect</radical>s, meaning it's extremely <kanji>strong</kanji>. Insect bodies are really durable, so they make for super strong bows. That's why you have to keep this one private — it's simply too strong to let others get ahold of.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You had this <kanji>strong</kanji> bow made especially for you in <reading>きょう</reading>と. きょうと is famous for their strong bows, so if you ever want another, you know where to go.
kanji.scene_hook: You keep your bow private because it's made of insects, meaning it's extremely strong.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A good <radical>leader</radical> can put the <radical>ground</radical>'s <radical>history</radical> to great <kanji>use</kanji>. You are a good leader. You have studied the ground and dirt of the earth your entire life. You know the history of the ground and you know how to use it to lead your people.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>use</kanji> the ground to raise <reading>shee</reading>p (し). When you were down there sniffing around you realized that your people will flourish best if you use this land for grazing and raising sheep.
kanji.scene_hook: A good leader can put the ground's history to great use.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You see something. It's walking towards you, but you need to see better. When the person comes close, you realize "That person is <reading>me</reading> (み)!"
kanji.scene_hook: The radical see and the kanji see are exactly the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Direct a <radical>tsunami</radical> into a <radical>canine</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical> to <kanji>decrease</kanji> the amount of water that strikes the surrounding area.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: After you and your canine <kanji>decrease</kanji> the tsunami's power, <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん) comes out to thank you. That tsunami was on track to wash away Genji's house, which would have forced him to majorly decrease his frivolous playboy activities.
kanji.scene_hook: Direct a tsunami into a canine's mouth to decrease the amount of water that strikes the surrounding area.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>lion</radical> drops a <radical>drop</radical> of saliva onto this <radical>person</radical> <kanji>from</kanji> above. The person is in a much worse place <kanji>compared with</kanji> where they were before, <kanji>since</kanji> they only just recently fell into this pit.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>From</kanji> above there is a loud caw, and a giant <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) swoops down, forcing the lion to back off. The eagle grabs the person with its talons, bringing him into the air! But now he's... in the air being carried by a giant eagle, <kanji>compared with</kanji> where he was before, is this really better?!
kanji.scene_hook: The lion drops a drop of saliva onto this person from above.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Of course when you find a toe <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, you want to know where the toe came from. When you examine it, you see a name written on there. This toe belongs to the local clumsy farmhand, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう).
kanji.scene_hook: You find a toe on the ground.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>enter</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>enter</kanji> as well.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Turns out, after you <kanji>enter</kanji> the tent (remember how the person in the radical is entering one?), you're given a <reading>new</reading> (にゅう) one! Why? No idea, but hey — it's new!
kanji.scene_hook: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for enter first!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You use a <radical>thread</radical> for <radical>good luck</radical> by <kanji>bind</kanji>ing two fingers together. You <kanji>tie</kanji> them nice and tight.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: After you <kanji>bind</kanji> your fingers, the good luck starts pouring in. Literally. Suddenly <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ) starts pouring down from the heavens.
kanji.scene_hook: You use a thread for good luck by binding two fingers together.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>fruit</radical> radical and the <kanji>fruit</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Bodacious!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: They sell <kanji>fruit</kanji> at the store, and you need to drive your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) to get there. A car is the fastest way to fruit.
kanji.scene_hook: The fruit radical and the fruit kanji are exactly the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You see an <radical>animal</radical> with some <radical>flowers</radical> and a <radical>turkey</radical> on a <radical>stool</radical> trying to <kanji>seize</kanji> them. The animal with the flowers is really tall so the turkey needs to stand on the stool in its attempt to seize the flowers.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The animal the turkey is trying to <kanji>seize</kanji> the flowers from is a <reading>cock</reading> (かく). The cock is a lot bigger than the turkey, so that’s why the turkey needs to stand on the stool in its attempt to seize those flowers.
kanji.scene_hook: You see an animal with some flowers and a turkey on a stool trying to seize them.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>cow</radical> with <radical>wing</radical>s is flying in the air. What a strange <kanji>thing</kanji>! You've heard of flying pigs before, but cows? This is a new and strange thing indeed! Cows with wings! What a silly thing.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You want a <kanji>thing</kanji> to help you fly with the cow so you put on your flying <reading>boots</reading> (ぶつ). Your flying boots are a pretty strange thing in themselves, but they're the only way you can keep up.
kanji.scene_hook: A cow with wings is flying in the air.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Construction</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical> leads to <kanji>aggression</kanji>. Just think about it. It's cold, it's unpleasant, and you don't want to be there. So, you start to develop an aggression towards other people working with you on this winter project.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You're taking your <kanji>aggression</kanji> out on everyone. Then, the sexy construction worker from WaniKani Level 1, <reading>こう</reading>いち, walks by without a shirt on. You take out all your aggression on him.
kanji.scene_hook: Construction in the winter leads to aggression.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If someone is coming toward you in a <radical>car</radical>, holding a <radical>weapon</radical> in their <radical>hand</radical>, they're going to <kanji>attack</kanji> you!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Right before the <kanji>attack</kanji> begins, you recognize the hand holding this weapon and driving this car. It's <reading>Gecki</reading> (げき), your long-lost gecko who you may or may not have let fly off into a really fierce tsunami. Oops. I think he may be here attacking you out of revenge.
kanji.scene_hook: If someone is coming toward you in a car, holding a weapon in their hand, they're going to attack you!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You have two <radical>bow</radical>s but they're both covered in <radical>ice</radical>. This has made them very <kanji>weak</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You take the bows out to your car, which is stuck in a bunch of ice and snow, and try to use them as a kind of <kanji>weak</kanji> <reading>jack</reading> (じゃく). You know, to get your car unstuck. Unfortunately, your bows are too weak and brittle to use as jacks, and they just snap in half.
kanji.scene_hook: You have two bows but they're both covered in ice.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: How many <radical>evenings</radical> are there? There are <kanji>many</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic:
To remember the reading た we use the word "taco."

Let's start with the kanji meaning: <kanji>many</kanji>. You go outside in the evening (because that's what this kanji is made up of) and are surprised when you're hit by <kanji>many</kanji> <reading>ta</reading>cos (た) flying from the sky. For some reason it's raining tacos, and of course you have no idea why.
kanji.scene_hook: How many evenings are there?

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>fins</radical> that fell onto my <radical>sword</radical> were sliced into many <kanji>part</kanji>s. Following this logic, this also means <kanji>minute</kanji>, which is part of an hour, and <kanji>divide</kanji>, which is the action of splitting something into many parts.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Now that the fins have been cut into neat <kanji>part</kanji>s, it's time to cut something else: <reading>bun</reading>s (ぶん).
kanji.scene_hook: The fins that fell onto my sword were sliced into many parts.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>ground</radical> is scattered with <radical>rice</radical>, which will cause the Predator to <kanji>come</kanji> because he loves rice. Then once he's gone, the <kanji>next</kanji> Predator will come to eat.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: When they come, though, they're all disappointed. It turns out it's <reading>rye</reading> (らい) grains, and rye grains taste terrible. Yuck.
kanji.scene_hook: The ground is scattered with rice, which will cause the Predator to come because he loves rice.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>private</radical> place on the <radical>moon</radical>, you hide two <radical>spoon</radical>s that give you a special <kanji>ability</kanji>. It's the ability to predict the future! The private moon spoons reflect the knowledge of time and space into your brain. Now that's a cool ability!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>ability</kanji> to predict the future makes you famous and now everyone knows your name: <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう)! And now that people know about your name and special ability, everyone will love you!
kanji.scene_hook: In a private place on the moon, you hide two spoons that give you a special ability.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are <kanji>power</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Who has the most power of anyone? It's the <kanji>Power</kanji> Rangers. They are battling their arch enemy <reading>Ricky</reading> (りき). They defeat him and <reading>lock</reading> (りょく) him up, so that he can't hurt anyone ever again.
kanji.scene_hook: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>not</radical> and the kanji <kanji>not</kanji> are the same thing! That makes it easy... Not!
kanji.reading_mnemonic:
To remember ふ we'll use the word "fool."

Think of those jokes, where someone says something, then ends with a loud "<kanji>NOTTTT</kanji>!" Someone is doing that to you, over and over. What is that person? That person is a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ).
kanji.scene_hook: The radical not and the kanji not are the same thing!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: With a <radical>roof</radical> over her head, a <radical>woman</radical> can <kanji>relax</kanji>. She doesn't have to worry about rain or snow or finding a place to sleep. The roof also happened to be super <kanji>cheap</kanji>, so she can even relax about her finances.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As she <kanji>relax</kanji>es, she pulls out the <kanji>cheap</kanji>est, most relaxing food in the world: <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん).
kanji.scene_hook: With a roof over her head, a woman can relax.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There are two <radical>mountains</radical> in front of you... and probably a bunch more around you too. You are in a pass, and don't know how to get out. You are frantically looking for the <kanji>exit</kanji> before you freeze to death. The most important thing is to find the exit from this mountain range - your life depends on it.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you're trying to <kanji>exit</kanji> this mountain range, you suddenly realize that someone is actually hunting you. What? Make sure to feel that sudden realization. It's crazy. Then, you find the exit and run towards it. Sadly, the hunter is waiting there, and he <reading>shoots you</reading> (しゅつ) through the leg, knocking you down.
kanji.scene_hook: There are two mountains in front of you...

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You use a <radical>thread</radical> for <radical>good luck</radical> by <kanji>bind</kanji>ing two fingers together. You <kanji>tie</kanji> them nice and tight.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: After you <kanji>bind</kanji> your fingers, the good luck starts pouring in. Literally. Suddenly <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ) starts pouring down from the heavens.
kanji.scene_hook: You use a thread for good luck by binding two fingers together.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> is what a <radical>lifeguard</radical> uses to <kanji>set up</kanji> the station where he'll <kanji>care</kanji> for the people on the beach and in the water. Trees provide the shade lifeguards need to take care of half-drowned kids and grannies with sand stuck in their bathing suits. That's why they set up their care stations here.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You look closer at the <kanji>set up</kanji> and realize the lifeguard is <reading>こう</reading>いち! You know he teaches Japanese but... is こういち really qualified to care for people like this?
kanji.scene_hook: A tree is what a lifeguard uses to set up the station where he'll care for the people on the beach and in the water.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Of course when you find a toe <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, you want to know where the toe came from. When you examine it, you see a name written on there. This toe belongs to the local clumsy farmhand, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう).
kanji.scene_hook: You find a toe on the ground.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You need to look a <radical>tree</radical> in the <radical>eye</radical> to gain a <kanji>mutual</kanji> respect for each other. Stand up straight, look the tree directly in its eye(s). If it looks right back, you know things are mutual.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: If your respect for the tree is <kanji>mutual</kanji> with the respect the tree feels for you, you'll be able to see the tree's <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Right now its soul looks pretty sad, like it's trying to say, "Why are your people cutting down all my friends? I thought we had a mutual understanding."
kanji.scene_hook: You need to look a tree in the eye to gain a mutual respect for each other.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>hand</radical> radical and the <kanji>hand</kanji> kanji are identical in looks and meaning! That should make things easy!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: To remember the reading て we will use the word "television." You have great hands. Go ahead and look at them. OH MY GOD. You have little <reading>te</reading>levisions (て) on your hands. They are playing the dumbest show you can think of, too, both hands at the same time.
kanji.scene_hook: The hand radical and the hand kanji are identical in looks and meaning!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There's an <radical>ear</radical> just sitting on a <radical>stool</radical>. Nobody's watching, so, you <kanji>take</kanji> it.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You're the kind of person who likes <vocabulary>to take</vocabulary> things. "What things?" you ask. Little <reading>toe</reading>s (と). Because that isn't creepy.
kanji.scene_hook: There's an ear just sitting on a stool.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around the <radical>sun</radical> <radical>temple</radical>, it's likely you'll <kanji>acquire</kanji> something. When you loiter around like that it looks like you're waiting for something, so if someone puts something in your hands and you just happen to acquire it, well, no one can fault you for that.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You managed to <kanji>acquire</kanji> some tobacco, but just as you're about to leave <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu comes marching up to you!! Or at least, his ghost does. He stares you dead in the eyes and says, "I didn't found the shogunate for you to acquire illegal goods and kill your body toking on my soil."
kanji.scene_hook: If you loiter around the sun temple, it's likely you'll acquire something.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you're riding your <radical>scooter</radical> through some <radical>rice</radical>, it's likely you've gone <kanji>astray</kanji>. Also you might be <kanji>lost</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You got so <kanji>lost</kanji> and went so <kanji>astray</kanji> you ended up on the <reading>May</reading>flower  (めい), a ship that eventually took you across the Atlantic Ocean. Now you've really, really gone astray.
kanji.scene_hook: If you're riding your scooter through some rice, it's likely you've gone astray.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: It may just be a <radical>roof</radical> over a <radical>bathtub</radical>, but this here is actually a <kanji>Shinto shrine</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: What god does this <kanji>Shinto shrine</kanji> hold? You see that "It's a-<reading>me-ya</reading> (みや)! (Mario!)"
kanji.scene_hook: It may just be a roof over a bathtub, but this here is actually a Shinto shrine.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You have to gather <radical>thread</radical> to <radical>sell</radical> if you want to <kanji>continue</kanji> living. You want to make a living, don't you? Make sure you continue to sell that thread every single day!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You have to <kanji>continue</kanji> to sell that thread because a Boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang is forcing you to do it. You see? If you want to continue to live, you have to appease this Bosozoku gang. You better keep selling!
kanji.scene_hook: You have to gather thread to sell if you want to continue living.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Dirt</radical> makes it <radical>easy</radical> to identify your <kanji>location</kanji>. Even if you add an extra line to mark the ground, it's still easy. Keep that in mind and you can go from <kanji>place</kanji> to place.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Knowing your <kanji>location</kanji> from dirt is quite a feat! Who taught you that? It was <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big local farmhand. Despite being a bit weird, he can identify any <kanji>place</kanji> just from its dirt!
kanji.scene_hook: Dirt makes it easy to identify your location.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You're tearing this <radical>door</radical> down with an <radical>axe</radical>. You're absolutely crazy. "I HATE THIS <kanji>PLACE</kanji>!!" you keep yelling as you smash in the door.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You smash through the door and finally get inside the <kanji>place</kanji>, only to find some kind of <reading>sho</reading>w (しょ) going on. It's a circus show — elephants, tightrope walkers, bears on unicycles! The greatest show on earth, but why is it at this place?
kanji.scene_hook: You're tearing this door down with an axe.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>canine</radical> puts its <radical>mouth</radical> on your <radical>heart</radical>, it's a pretty unforgettable <kanji>feeling</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Overall, though, it's a good <kanji>feeling</kanji> — so good that you feel like Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! With this canine lovingly slobbering on your heart, you feel like you could go out and conquer the world!
kanji.scene_hook: When a canine puts its mouth on your heart, it's a pretty unforgettable feeling.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you go up to a <radical>gate</radical> and press your <radical>ear</radical> to it, you'll <kanji>hear</kanji> what's going on on the other side. The people on the other side will have no idea you can hear their conversation.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: From the other side of the gate, you <kanji>hear</kanji> one of the people start to play a song on a recorder. You recognize the song as "Hot Cross <reading>Bun</reading>s (ぶん)." You haven't heard this song since grade school. "Hot cross buns, hot cross buns~"
kanji.scene_hook: If you go up to a gate and press your ear to it, you'll hear what's going on on the other side.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You use your <radical>scooter</radical> when the <radical>omen</radical> comes so that you can <kanji>escape</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You decide to <kanji>escape</kanji> this omen by weaving in and out of the busy streets of <reading>とう</reading>きょう. By now, you know とうきょう like the back of your hand, so you're sure you can escape this way.
kanji.scene_hook: You use your scooter when the omen comes so that you can escape.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> is <radical>blue</radical>. As in, it's sad and not feeling well. This is because your soul changes colors depending on your <kanji>feeling</kanji>s or what <kanji>emotion</kanji>s you're expressing.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Why is your soul <kanji>feeling</kanji> blue? Because <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big local farmhand, was extremely mean to you.
kanji.scene_hook: Your soul is blue.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You want to share <radical>happiness</radical> with people so you <radical>stamp</radical> <radical>stool</radical>s with the <kanji>news</kanji>. Now when people want a <kanji>report</kanji> of all the happy things going on, they can just look down before they sit.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You research and <kanji>report</kanji> the <kanji>news</kanji> from <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Only after, once all of the news is stamped onto the stools, do you leave your home.
kanji.scene_hook: You want to share happiness with people so you stamp stools with the news.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: These days <radical>energy</radical> is the greatest <radical>treasure</radical>. Not gold, money, or anything like that. Imagine finding some in a literal treasure chest. As soon as you open it, out bursts the <kanji>energy</kanji> in a kind of <kanji>spirit</kanji> ball.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you reach inside the <kanji>energy</kanji> <kanji>spirit</kanji> ball, you feel something hard inside. It's a large <reading>key</reading> (き) that gives you access to another treasure.
kanji.scene_hook: These days energy is the greatest treasure.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Again</radical> and again, <radical>winter</radical> <kanji>change</kanji>s you each year.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You know what else <kanji>change</kanji>s each winter? <reading>Hen</reading>s (へん)! They're constantly laying eggs, which then (possibly) turn into new hens.
kanji.scene_hook: Again and again, winter changes you each year.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>say</radical> radical and the <kanji>say</kanji> kanji are the same!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: If you aren't sure what to <kanji>say</kanji>, just think about <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん) and ask yourself, "What would Genji say?"
kanji.scene_hook: The say radical and the say kanji are the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: All the <radical>flowers</radical> in the <radical>world</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s owe their existence to the humble <kanji>leaf</kanji>. That's because leaves are how trees, and therefore flowers, get their nutrients from the sun.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Picture a little flower working really hard to grow its first leaf. The <kanji>leaf</kanji> pops out and it shouts, "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)!”
kanji.scene_hook: All the flowers in the world of trees owe their existence to the humble leaf.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you know the radical meaning <radical>orders</radical> you know the kanji meaning <kanji>orders</kanji> as well!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>orders</kanji> are to get a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun.
kanji.scene_hook: If you know the radical meaning orders you know the kanji meaning orders as well!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> who is a <radical>nurse</radical> should be called <kanji>miss</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: "Call me <kanji>Miss</kanji> <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう)," Joe says to you.
kanji.scene_hook: A woman who is a nurse should be called miss.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>giant</radical> radical and the <kanji>giant</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Massive!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Just recently, a <kanji>giant</kanji> rampaged through Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ), so now it's even smaller than before. Poor Little Kyoto.
kanji.scene_hook: The giant radical and the giant kanji are exactly the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>big</radical> radical and the <kanji>big</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: There's this big guy (that's how you remember the radical, remember?), and he's wearing a huge <reading>Tie Dye</reading> (たい, だい) shirt. Because he's so huge, the tie dye shirt is also super huge. Everything about him is pretty big, but the tie dye stands out more than anything.
kanji.scene_hook: The big radical and the big kanji are exactly the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: When you take your <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>measurement</radical>s, you realize they're too short, so you <kanji>attach</kanji> things to them to make the measurements more impressive. Attach some heels to the shoes, then attach another head on top of theirs. There — perfect measurements thanks to these attachments.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You have to remember the kun'yomi reading of this kanji, and the part you need to remember is つ. So, think of how you would <kanji>attach</kanji> something to something else. What would you need? You'd need <reading>two</reading> (つ) things to put together!
kanji.scene_hook: When you take your leader's measurements, you realize they're too short, so you attach things to them to make the measurements more impressive.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: That <radical>slide</radical> is your <radical>husband</radical>'s only <kanji>fault</kanji>. He loves that slide, more than you, and it's the only thing you don't like about him.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You try to come up with another <kanji>fault</kanji> of his so you can complain, when you see a tiny <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ) trotting by. Oh no! Before you can call out, your husband has fallen off the slide and crushed the poor thing. You hear a sharp yelp and then nothing.
kanji.scene_hook: That slide is your husband's only fault.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>shellfish</radical> that can't live through <radical>winter</radical> is a <kanji>failure</kanji>. Any proper shellfish would survive through the winter, so this is quite an embarrassing <kanji>defeat</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The best way to avoid <kanji>failure</kanji> is to say a little <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい) to distract you from the cold. In fact, this can work in any situation where you're afraid of defeat. Say a haiku aloud, it will calm your nerves and warm you from the inside.
kanji.scene_hook: A shellfish that can't live through winter is a failure.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Inside this <radical>moon</radical> is a <radical>black hole</radical>... haha just kidding, it's your <kanji>belly</kanji>! It's beautiful and round like the moon and it eats up everything you give it like a black hole. What a good <kanji>stomach</kanji> you have!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: If you don't treat your belly with respect, when you look down at it, it'll yell, "F**k (ふく) you!" Whoa, looks like someone's got an upset stomach.
kanji.scene_hook: Inside this moon is a black hole...

kanji.meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>sound</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> gives me an <kanji>idea</kanji>. The methodic thump thump thumping of a heart puts me in a trance that gives me great ideas. It’s a very inspirational sound, the sound of a heart is. It gives me an idea. 

Alternatively, you could look at this kanji and see a "stand" radical and something that kind of (almost) looks like the kanji for "think." When you stand up and think, you come up with your very own <kanji>idea</kanji>s!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The heart of an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) is very strong. The person who knew this probably came up with the <kanji>idea</kanji> to make the bald eagle a symbol of the United States. Eagles are always flying around and doing things, so their hearts are very strong.
kanji.scene_hook: The sound of your heart gives me an idea.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> there is a <radical>jet</radical> dropping <kanji>flavor</kanji> bombs. Every time you <kanji>taste</kanji> something, it's actually because a tiny jet has flown into your mouth to bomb you with flavor.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: And what has more <kanji>flavor</kanji> than a big slab of <reading>me</reading>at (み)? You love meat and its flavor nearly as much as you love the <kanji>taste</kanji> of its juice. So many good things to put in your mouth!
kanji.scene_hook: In your mouth there is a jet dropping flavor bombs.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic:
The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>book</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>book</kanji> as well.

Take note that the second, third, and fourth meanings of this kanji are <kanji>origin</kanji>, <kanji>real</kanji>, and <kanji>main</kanji>. You can figure this out by thinking about how books mainly have their origins in real life.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Book</kanji>s are one of the best ways to <reading>hone</reading> (ほん) your intellect, you know?
kanji.scene_hook: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for book first!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: What superhero is most like a <radical>triceratops</radical>? …If you said <radical>Wolverine</radical>, you're <kanji>correct</kanji>! Why is that the <kanji>right</kanji> answer? Because the triceratops has three horns, and Wolverine has three blades coming out of his hands. It's obvious, isn't it? But you knew that of course.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Because you got the answer <kanji>correct</kanji>, you win a free trip to <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Congrats!
kanji.scene_hook: What superhero is most like a triceratops?

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>mix</radical> different types of <radical>power</radical> you'll produce something <kanji>effective</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The most <kanji>effective</kanji> mix of two powers is two <reading>こう</reading>いちs. Take one, mix it with another, and you have something with immeasurable power. You use it to attack that animal-like creature. It's super effective!
kanji.scene_hook: If you mix different types of power you'll produce something effective.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>fruit</radical> radical and the <kanji>fruit</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Bodacious!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: They sell <kanji>fruit</kanji> at the store, and you need to drive your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) to get there. A car is the fastest way to fruit.
kanji.scene_hook: The fruit radical and the fruit kanji are exactly the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A good <radical>leader</radical> can put the <radical>ground</radical>'s <radical>history</radical> to great <kanji>use</kanji>. You are a good leader. You have studied the ground and dirt of the earth your entire life. You know the history of the ground and you know how to use it to lead your people.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>use</kanji> the ground to raise <reading>shee</reading>p (し). When you were down there sniffing around you realized that your people will flourish best if you use this land for grazing and raising sheep.
kanji.scene_hook: A good leader can put the ground's history to great use.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>winter</radical> <radical>tree</radical> is only a winter tree due to a <kanji>clause</kanji> in its tree contract.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>clause</kanji> was written into all trees' contracts by <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the large farmhand.
kanji.scene_hook: A winter tree is only a winter tree due to a clause in its tree contract.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>cow</radical> tribe is too important to deal with small <kanji>matter</kanji>s and trivial <kanji>affair</kanji>s.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: All of his <kanji>affair</kanji>s are taken care of by <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai, who he hired as his underling.
kanji.scene_hook: The leader of the cow tribe is too important to deal with small matters and trivial affairs.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>mouth</radical> inside a <radical>mouth</radical>. But if you look even closer (not actually, but we're pretending), you can see another mouth inside that mouth... and another one inside of that one! It keeps going and going. Can you count the number of <kanji>times</kanji> there's a mouth inside a mouth? This kanji also means <kanji>revolve</kanji> too. Think of each "time" a thing happens, it loops back around and comes back to the beginning, revolving around again another time.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: If you're having a hard time wrapping your head around the infinite number of <kanji>times</kanji> these mouths <kanji>revolve</kanji>, just picture yourself in a little <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) going around and down through the mouths.
kanji.scene_hook: There's a mouth inside a mouth.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> around a <radical>black hole</radical>, waiting for it to finish destroying and switch to <kanji>restore</kanji> mode. You were told all black holes finish eventually, and when they do you can press a button to restore everything back to the way it was.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Finally there's nothing else for the black hole to eat so you hit the <kanji>restore</kanji> button.... but nothing happens. "Oh <reading>f**k</reading> (ふく) you!" you scream as you realize you were lied to.
kanji.scene_hook: You loiter around a black hole, waiting for it to finish destroying and switch to restore mode.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>narwhal</radical> <radical>stick</radical>s a <radical>child</radical> with its horn then you have to admit that it <kanji>exist</kanji>s. We know some of you have been doubting narwhals and whether they're real. Well now your kid is a kabob, so do you believe they exist now? I <kanji>suppose</kanji> you do.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Oh, and the child we had to narwhal stab to prove they <kanji>exist</kanji> - that's your <reading>son</reading> (そん). Maybe you should have listened and kept your son out of the Narwhal Danger <reading>Zone</reading> (ぞん) after we warned you about it.
kanji.scene_hook: If a narwhal sticks a child with its horn then you have to admit that it exists.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>narwhal</radical> sticks a <radical>stick</radical> into the <radical>dirt</radical> to see what might <kanji>exist</kanji> underneath it. Narwhals live in the sea, you see. So they're convinced nothing can exist anywhere else.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The narwhal can't find anything but it can't stop thinking about what could <kanji>exist</kanji> outside of its world. It pulls out a <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい) and starts tapping the keys with its horn. It plays a sad song about the meaning of life and whether existence is worth it.
kanji.scene_hook: A narwhal sticks a stick into the dirt to see what might exist underneath it.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic:
A <radical>dirt</radical> <radical>alligator</radical> lives in the <kanji>earth</kanji>, not the water. They can swim through the earth itself! Which is pretty scary.

You can think of this word as <kanji>ground</kanji> too, because that's kind of what earth is made of.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The only way to keep this dirt alligator from dragging you under the <kanji>earth</kanji> and eating you is to feed it <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). That way it won't lead you to a horrific death under the earth by dragging you under and spinning you around until you eat dirt (literally).
kanji.scene_hook: A dirt alligator lives in the earth, not the water.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> <radical>request</radical>s a <kanji>sphere</kanji>!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>sphere</kanji> is a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) sphere. A strange sphere shaped cucumber.
kanji.scene_hook: The king requests a sphere!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> radical and the <kanji>life</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You can save <kanji>life</kanji> or take it away with your new <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). This is the saber you got from doing the correct thing (remember?), and now you need to figure out how to use it with this life. So what do you do?
kanji.scene_hook: The life radical and the life kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> radical and the <kanji>life</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You can save <kanji>life</kanji> or take it away with your new <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). This is the saber you got from doing the correct thing (remember?), and now you need to figure out how to use it with this life. So what do you do?
kanji.scene_hook: The life radical and the life kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>child</radical> radical and the <kanji>child</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.
kanji.reading_mnemonic:
The main reading here is し, so to remember it we'll use this mnemonic:

There is a <kanji>child</kanji> standing in front of you. You look closely at the child but something seems wrong. You rub your eyes and when you open them again, there is a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in front of you. A sheep?! You yelp, blink again, and it's back to being a child. Are… are all children really just sheep?
kanji.scene_hook: The child radical and the child kanji are exactly the same.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>leader</radical> wants to work <radical>together</radical>, it means they want what you have to <kanji>offer</kanji>. Congrats! Now you can <kanji>accompany</kanji> them everywhere and offer them hot towels and bottled water, or whatever it is you do…
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Once a year, the leader will <kanji>offer</kanji> you a vacation in <reading>きょう</reading>と. It's an all-expenses-paid trip! The catch is that you have to <kanji>accompany</kanji> your leader to きょうと…
kanji.scene_hook: When a leader wants to work together, it means they want what you have to offer.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>lid</radical> on your <radical>mouth</radical> and <radical>mustache</radical>, covering up all your talking holes. This is because you kept saying how <kanji>tall</kanji> and <kanji>expensive</kanji> you are.
kanji.reading_mnemonic:
"I am <kanji>tall</kanji>er and more <kanji>expensive</kanji> than you." 

You're saying this because there are some <reading>こう</reading>いちs in the room, and you want to make them feel inferior (you're a こういち hater). You keep comparing your height to the こういちs in the room, and you keep checking price tags. Then you tell them you're taller and more expensive than they are.
kanji.scene_hook: There's a lid on your mouth and mustache, covering up all your talking holes.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Go to the <radical>tent</radical> and take the <radical>lantern</radical> from it, then we will be ready for <kanji>departure</kanji>. We can't leave the lantern inside of the tent; that's a fire hazard. We'd best take it with us before the departure. Don't you agree?
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Oh! Don't forget your <reading>hats</reading> (はつ) either. Wouldn’t want to start a <kanji>departure</kanji> without hats or a lantern. That'd be a short journey.
kanji.scene_hook: Go to the tent and take the lantern from it, then we will be ready for departure.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You see something. It's walking towards you, but you need to see better. When the person comes close, you realize "That person is <reading>me</reading> (み)!"
kanji.scene_hook: The radical see and the kanji see are exactly the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You wear a special <radical>outfit</radical> and equip a <radical>sword</radical> whenever you do something for the <kanji>first</kanji> time. Doing something for the first time is always scary, but the right outfit and a sword at your side can give you the confidence and courage to overcome your jitters!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: So, what is it you're doing for the <kanji>first</kanji> time? A fashion <reading>show</reading> (しょ). Your special outfit and sword aren't just to calm your nerves — they're also what you'll be showing off on the catwalk.
kanji.scene_hook: You wear a special outfit and equip a sword whenever you do something for the first time.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Someone throws a <radical>cleat</radical> at your <radical>forehead</radical> while you sit on a <radical>stool</radical>. You must <kanji>accept</kanji> this. Then another guy cleats you in the forehead, but you remain on the stool and <kanji>receive</kanji> it without complaint. This is some kind of acceptance ritual for the most prestigious Spanish soccer club in the world. If a cleat to the forehead doesn't knock you off the stool, you'll receive an offer to join.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: After they <kanji>accept</kanji> you, they give you a small <reading>ju</reading>ice (じゅ) box. It's to help you recover from the trauma of getting kicked. And you <kanji>receive</kanji> the juice with grace.
kanji.scene_hook: Someone throws a cleat at your forehead while you sit on a stool.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The radical for <radical>eat</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>eat</kanji> are the same! Lucky you.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Thank goodness, you finally get to <kanji>eat</kanji> your <kanji>meal</kanji>. You're so hungry. Someone has been starving you and now they've finally given you food. Unfortunately, though, every time you take a bite, you get a <reading>shock</reading> (しょく) from somewhere.
kanji.scene_hook: The radical for eat and the kanji for eat are the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you're sitting there next to <kanji>One</kanji>, holding him up, you start feeling a weird sensation all over your skin. From the wound comes a fine powder (obviously coming from the special bullet used to kill One) that causes the person it touches to get extremely <reading>itchy</reading> (いち).
kanji.scene_hook: Lying on the ground is something that looks just like the ground, the number One.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: With a <radical>lip ring</radical>, anything is <kanji>possible</kanji>. Having a lip ring grants you the power to conquer any obstacle, achieve any goal. As long as it's a <kanji>passable</kanji> lip ring, that is. If it isn't good enough, it isn't possible to be passable.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Since anything and everything is <kanji>possible</kanji> with your new <kanji>passable</kanji> lip ring, you use it to get a new <reading>ca</reading>r (か). You go to the car dealership and ask for a free (passable) car. The manager is impressed with your lip ring and gladly hands over the keys to the latest model of your favorite car. Not bad.
kanji.scene_hook: With a lip ring, anything is possible.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>private</radical> place on the <radical>moon</radical>, you hide two <radical>spoon</radical>s that give you a special <kanji>ability</kanji>. It's the ability to predict the future! The private moon spoons reflect the knowledge of time and space into your brain. Now that's a cool ability!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>ability</kanji> to predict the future makes you famous and now everyone knows your name: <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう)! And now that people know about your name and special ability, everyone will love you!
kanji.scene_hook: In a private place on the moon, you hide two spoons that give you a special ability.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>tree</radical> and your <radical>hat</radical> protects you from the <radical>water</radical>, but the hat itself is now covered in <kanji>lacquer</kanji>. You take it off and see that it's shiny and hard and protected.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You decide to scoop up the rest of the <kanji>lacquer</kanji> water and use it on your furniture to protect it from your <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ). Now it can't bite or scratch or ruin any of your things anymore!
kanji.scene_hook: A tsunami hits a tree and your hat protects you from the water, but the hat itself is now covered in lacquer.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>original</radical> radical and the <kanji>original</kanji> kanji are the same!

This kanji can also mean <kanji>field</kanji>, probably because that's what everything originally looked like before humans: just a bunch of fields.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>original</kanji> Japanese hero was <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん), from "The Tale of Genji."
kanji.scene_hook: The original radical and the original kanji are the same!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you have a <radical>bow</radical> and a <radical>stick</radical> you need to <kanji>pull</kanji> the two back together to shoot it. If you can't pull it, you won't be shooting anything.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: When you <kanji>pull</kanji> the bow back, your body strains and you feel an immense <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) rise inside of you. You keep pulling and the heat grows hotter and hotter.
kanji.scene_hook: If you have a bow and a stick you need to pull the two back together to shoot it.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>simple</radical> <radical>drunkard</radical> starting a <kanji>war</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The simple drunkard started a <kanji>war</kanji> with the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん)! This is especially unfortunate, because the centaurs are really good at <kanji>battle</kanji>s because they're all automatically cavalry troops.
kanji.scene_hook: A simple drunkard starting a war.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You have a <radical>small</radical> <radical>slide</radical>. The thing about small slides is that <kanji>few</kanji> people can use them. Not everyone can fit their bum in a small slide — only a few small ones can make it down.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Another reason so <kanji>few</kanji> people can use it is that the <reading>Sho</reading>gun (しょう) is trying to go down the slide. We're talking a grown man who's way too big for this little slide, so a few people have to wait behind him — it's a slide traffic jam.
kanji.scene_hook: You have a small slide.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Let the <radical>sun</radical> shine into your <radical>ear</radical> while sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> and make sure <kanji>most</kanji> of it gets in your head. You want to fill your brain with the most sun anyone ever has.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Most</kanji> of this is for your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). You're testing out a new way to power it, but if you can't get most of the sun into your ear, it won't be enough to power most of the cyborg's robo-parts.
kanji.scene_hook: Let the sun shine into your ear while sitting on a stool and make sure most of it gets in your head.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around to <radical>poop</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> you never know what could be happening <kanji>behind</kanji> you. <kanji>After</kanji> you're done you could turn <kanji>back</kanji> to see something really crazy that you missed because you took so long to poop in the cold.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You finally look <kanji>behind</kanji> you and see <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) and a giant <reading>こう</reading>いち having a violent battle <kanji>back</kanji> there!
kanji.scene_hook: If you loiter around to poop in winter you never know what could be happening behind you.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you're sitting there next to <kanji>One</kanji>, holding him up, you start feeling a weird sensation all over your skin. From the wound comes a fine powder (obviously coming from the special bullet used to kill One) that causes the person it touches to get extremely <reading>itchy</reading> (いち).
kanji.scene_hook: Lying on the ground is something that looks just like the ground, the number One.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You have to <radical>sickle</radical> the <radical>rice paddy</radical> today because it's your <kanji>turn</kanji>. This kanji also means <kanji>number in a series</kanji>, which is a lot like a turn. For example, you're number 3, so your turn will be number 3.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>turn</kanji>-based rice sickle system is a contest as well! But, you are disappointed to find out that you have been <reading>ban</reading>ned (ばん) from the game because of the performance enhancing steroids you've been taking.
kanji.scene_hook: You have to sickle the rice paddy today because it's your turn.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>line up</radical> your body with the <radical>sun</radical> and everything looks <kanji>normal</kanji>.  The sun is lined up behind you, and you’re looking at all the things the sun has lit up. Everything looks normal.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Soon you see something that doesn’t look so <kanji>normal</kanji>. It’s a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ), a jester. Dressed all in fool’s garb, this fool is dancing up towards you with a ridiculously unsettling grin on his face.
kanji.scene_hook: You line up your body with the sun and everything looks normal.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>scooter</radical> with your <radical>mama</radical> carrying a <radical>task</radical> she needs to get done. You see her <kanji>pass through</kanji>, hurrying by to get that task done.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you watch your mama <kanji>pass through</kanji> the house, she yells, "YOU HAVE TO DO THIS TASK <reading>TOO</reading> (つう)!" Too?! Oh no, now you have to do it too. Hurry up!
kanji.scene_hook: There's a scooter with your mama carrying a task she needs to get done.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>scooter</radical> with your <radical>mama</radical> carrying a <radical>task</radical> she needs to get done. You see her <kanji>pass through</kanji>, hurrying by to get that task done.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: As you watch your mama <kanji>pass through</kanji> the house, she yells, "YOU HAVE TO DO THIS TASK <reading>TOO</reading> (つう)!" Too?! Oh no, now you have to do it too. Hurry up!
kanji.scene_hook: There's a scooter with your mama carrying a task she needs to get done.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you've been walking all day, your <radical>foot</radical> is the last thing anyone would want to <radical>kiss</radical>, especially after wandering barefoot down a <kanji>road</kanji>. Your feet are filthy, sweaty, and stinky. Nobody is going to want to kiss those feet.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: What was this person walking down again? Oh, that's right – a <reading>ro</reading>ad (ろ). They were walking down a filthy road.
kanji.scene_hook: If you've been walking all day, your foot is the last thing anyone would want to kiss, especially after wandering barefoot down a road.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: From the <radical>roof</radical>, an <radical>icicle</radical> falls and hits <radical>Wolverine</radical> in the <radical>forehead</radical>, and he has to go over to the <radical>stool</radical> to <kanji>lie down</kanji>. And eventually he goes to <kanji>sleep</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: While wolverine <kanji>sleep</kanji>s, he makes a horrible <reading>neigh</reading>ing (ね) sound. It's like there's a giant horse in his mouth screaming to get out! NEIGH NEIGH!
kanji.scene_hook: From the roof, an icicle falls and hits Wolverine in the forehead, and he has to go over to the stool to lie down.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>roof</radical> falls on you right between your <radical>rib cage</radical> and your <radical>mouth</radical>, slicing through you like a <radical>knife</radical> and managing to completely <kanji>divide</kanji> your body in two.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Who caused this roof to fall on you and <kanji>divide</kanji> your body in two? <reading>Wari</reading>o (わり) and his <reading>wa</reading>lrus (わ) sidekick. Wario's always been a bad guy, but since teaming up with his walrus buddy, he's gotten into some really twisted stuff… like using roofs to divide people in half, guillotine-style.
kanji.scene_hook: A roof falls on you right between your rib cage and your mouth, slicing through you like a knife and managing to completely divide your body in two.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>mustache</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. Upon closer inspection, you see that the mustache on the ground is exactly the <kanji>same</kanji> as yours!
kanji.reading_mnemonic:
When we need to remember the reading どう, we use the word "doughnut".

While this is already strange enough, you notice something stranger. Over a couple feet away is a <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう) who also has the <kanji>same</kanji> mustache as you (and the one that fell on the ground). The doughnut looks at you for a moment, then scampers off. This doughnut must be a mutant because normally doughnuts don't move.
kanji.scene_hook: There's a mustache on the ground.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>sun</radical> is used in a <radical>temple</radical> to tell <kanji>time</kanji>. They don't have clocks or cell phones, they have sun dials and good old fashioned "looking up and seeing where the sun is right now."

This also can mean <kanji>o' clock</kanji>, as in "five o'clock" It's used when measuring what time it is in Japanese.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Time</kanji> in a temple makes you think of <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). Specifically, Jesus being able to perform time travel magic. He winks at you, goes back to the dinosaur ages, where he wrestles a T-rex. Then, he goes to WWII era and punches Hitler in the mouth.
kanji.scene_hook: The sun is used in a temple to tell time.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Below the <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s is a <radical>demon</radical>. This demon is a <kanji>devil</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Why is the <kanji>devil</kanji> under a canopy of trees? Because he's looking for his <reading>ma</reading>ma (ま).
kanji.scene_hook: Below the canopy of trees is a demon.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are <kanji>power</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Who has the most power of anyone? It's the <kanji>Power</kanji> Rangers. They are battling their arch enemy <reading>Ricky</reading> (りき). They defeat him and <reading>lock</reading> (りょく) him up, so that he can't hurt anyone ever again.
kanji.scene_hook: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Let the <radical>sun</radical> shine into your <radical>ear</radical> while sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> and make sure <kanji>most</kanji> of it gets in your head. You want to fill your brain with the most sun anyone ever has.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Most</kanji> of this is for your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). You're testing out a new way to power it, but if you can't get most of the sun into your ear, it won't be enough to power most of the cyborg's robo-parts.
kanji.scene_hook: Let the sun shine into your ear while sitting on a stool and make sure most of it gets in your head.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You wear a special <radical>outfit</radical> and equip a <radical>sword</radical> whenever you do something for the <kanji>first</kanji> time. Doing something for the first time is always scary, but the right outfit and a sword at your side can give you the confidence and courage to overcome your jitters!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: So, what is it you're doing for the <kanji>first</kanji> time? A fashion <reading>show</reading> (しょ). Your special outfit and sword aren't just to calm your nerves — they're also what you'll be showing off on the catwalk.
kanji.scene_hook: You wear a special outfit and equip a sword whenever you do something for the first time.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> and <radical>ice</radical> has brought this year to its <kanji>end</kanji>. Once you're in the long thread of winter and ice, you know the year will end soon.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Whenever winter <kanji>end</kanji>s, you take the thread covered in ice, and lace it into your <reading>shoe</reading> (しゅう).
kanji.scene_hook: The thread of winter and ice has brought this year to its end.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Who's someone who's just a <radical>mouth</radical> with <radical>legs</radical>? That's your know-it-all <kanji>older brother</kanji>, who won't shut up about anything.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You wish you could box your loud-mouth <kanji>older brother</kanji> up and send him far away to <reading>きょう</reading>と. きょうと is super far away from where you currently live, so just ship your older bro off so he can never bother you again.
kanji.scene_hook: Who's someone who's just a mouth with legs?

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There is someone wearing <radical>horns</radical>, holding a <radical>bow</radical>, and going down a <radical>slide</radical> held up by a <radical>stick</radical>. What type of person do you imagine? I'd say someone who's an immature boy. That would be your <kanji>younger brother</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>younger brother</kanji> loves playing dress-up so much that he's now started <reading>dye</reading>ing (だい) his hair all kinds of colors.
kanji.scene_hook: There is someone wearing horns, holding a bow, and going down a slide held up by a stick.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> to <radical>oneself</radical> (yourself), you decide to <kanji>write down</kanji>. Whether this is in the form of a journal, diary, or blog, you feel the need to <kanji>record</kanji> these important thoughts in some way.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You need to protect what you <kanji>write down</kanji> and <kanji>record</kanji> for yourself with a <reading>key</reading> (き). These are your special thoughts and feelings, you don't want anyone else to see! Lock them away with a key!
kanji.scene_hook: What you say to oneself (yourself), you decide to write down.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: When your <radical>soul</radical> has an <radical>idea</radical> it's just a <kanji>recollection</kanji> of something you already know. Only your brain can have original ideas, so when your soul tries to come up with one, it's just a recollection of something you thought before now.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Your soul is sending you a <kanji>recollection</kanji> right now of... your past life as a farmer in <reading>Ok</reading>lahoma (おく). You see yourself farming and sweating and living a wonderful life. Hmm, maybe you should move to Oklahoma and live the simple life again.
kanji.scene_hook: When your soul has an idea it's just a recollection of something you already know.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you give a <radical>woman</radical> a <radical>machine</radical> she'll <kanji>begin</kanji> learning how to use it right away. She'll also get it to <kanji>start</kanji> before anyone else could. Women are really good at starting up new machines!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: Actually, before she can <kanji>begin</kanji>, she has to shear the <reading>shee</reading>p (し) over there. The machine is going to turn wool into yarn, but you can't just start that process by shoving the sheep in there. You have to keep the little sheep safe!
kanji.scene_hook: If you give a woman a machine she'll begin learning how to use it right away.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>grain</radical> seems extra <radical>heavy</radical>, you know it's a different <kanji>kind</kanji>. Not all <kanji>type</kanji>s of grains are the same. So when you feel the heavy type, you know it isn't the same as the light kind.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You sort all of the different <kanji>kind</kanji>s and <kanji>type</kanji>s by putting them in the right <reading>chu</reading>tes (しゅ). They slide away, probably to some big type-based piles you can't see.
kanji.scene_hook: If a grain seems extra heavy, you know it's a different kind.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If someone is headed in your <radical>direction</radical> with a <radical>gun</radical>, nock an <radical>arrow</radical> in your bow and gather your <kanji>tribe</kanji> behind you because it’s about to get real! This man with a gun is headed your direction and he doesn’t even know what you and your tribe are gonna do to him. If he gets any closer, you’re likely to shoot that arrow right at him.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: When he starts to get closer, you notice that this guy is a boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang member. Bosozoku means “running <kanji>tribe</kanji>” and this guy is running right at you. With a gun. So gather your own tribe behind you and prepare to defend yourself against this bosozoku member.
kanji.scene_hook: If someone is headed in your direction with a gun, nock an arrow in your bow and gather your tribe behind you because it’s about to get real!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: You take your <radical>scooter</radical> to <radical>Korea</radical> and everything is <kanji>different</kanji>!
kanji.reading_mnemonic: What's also <kanji>different</kanji> is that there's a weird tiger bouncing on its tail alongside you yelling that its name is <reading>Tigger</reading> (ちが). You didn't know Tigger was living in Korea these days. That's definitely different.
kanji.scene_hook: You take your scooter to Korea and everything is different!

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>canopy</radical> you use in <radical>private</radical> is very <kanji>wide</kanji>.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You like being <kanji>wide</kanji> because it makes you feel like a different person. But not just any person, like a super <reading>hero</reading> (ひろ)!
kanji.scene_hook: The canopy you use in private is very wide.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you go up to a <radical>gate</radical> and press your <radical>ear</radical> to it, you'll <kanji>hear</kanji> what's going on on the other side. The people on the other side will have no idea you can hear their conversation.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: From the other side of the gate, you <kanji>hear</kanji> one of the people start to play a song on a recorder. You recognize the song as "Hot Cross <reading>Bun</reading>s (ぶん)." You haven't heard this song since grade school. "Hot cross buns, hot cross buns~"
kanji.scene_hook: If you go up to a gate and press your ear to it, you'll hear what's going on on the other side.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical>'s <radical>village</radical> is the <kanji>reason</kanji> he's king. Without the village there would be no reason to have a king in the first place.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: You can't have <kanji>reason</kanji> without <reading>re</reading> (り).
kanji.scene_hook: The king's village is the reason he's king.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A giant <radical>cross</radical> with a <radical>mouth</radical> stands above you. "I am <kanji>reason</kanji>!" it says to you. It is the Cross of Reason.
kanji.reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>reason</kanji> that this Cross of Reason exists is to combat the <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) of the world, who just bring chaos into everything.
kanji.scene_hook: A giant cross with a mouth stands above you.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> radical and <kanji>sun</kanji> kanji are the same, huzzah! Also, if you want to remember <kanji>day</kanji> as well, you can just think about how a day is the time it takes for the sun to cross the sky.
